items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands so the body keeps adapting.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible immediately.
items.why: Students think 'overload' means 'maximum'. It means slightly exceeding current capacity - too much causes injury; too little stalls progress. The key word is PROGRESSIVE.
Students confuse storage with circulation. Blood carries glucose; muscles and liver store glycogen (the storage form). Liver glycogen regulates blood sugar; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction.
The answer examiners want is: Only the flat side. “Both sides of stick” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Inside the D. “Anywhere” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many players in a field hockey team?
items.why: The real figure is 11. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
